perturbation.original_text: The term "Representatives" means each party's affiliates and such party's and its affiliates' directors, officers, partners, employees, agents and advisors (including, without limitation, financial advisors, legal counsel and accountants), and the term "person" shall include any corporation, company, partnership, limited liability company, trust, governmental entity or individual.
perturbation.changed_text: The term "Representatives" means only the directors, officers and employees of each party. The term "person" shall include only individual human beings, specifically excluding any corporations, companies, partnerships, limited liability companies, trusts or governmental entities.
perturbation.explanation: The modified text limits the definition of 'Representatives' and 'person' to exclude entities and advisors, conflicting with the common legal understanding and usage, and effectively shielding those entities from liability under the agreement.
perturbation.law_explanation: This statute states that 'person' includes corporations, companies, associations, firms, partnerships, societies, and joint stock companies, as well as individuals. The modified text explicitly excludes all of these, directly contradicting the federal definition and narrowing the scope of liability under the contract.

Words denoting number, gender, and so forth In determining the meaning of any Act of Congress, unless the context indicates otherwise- words importing the singular include and apply to several persons, parties, or things; words importing the plural include the singular; words importing the masculine gender include the feminine as well; words used in the present tense include the future as well as the present; the words "insane" and "insane person
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: 1 U.S. Code § 1 - Words denoting number, gender, and so forth | U.S. Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you Quick search by citation: Title Section Go! 1 U.S. Code § 1 - Words denoting number, gender, and so forth U.S. Code Notes prev | next In determining the meaning of any Act of Congress , unless the context indicates otherwise— words importing the singular include and apply to several persons , parties, or things; words importing the plural include the singular; words importing the masculine gender include the feminine as well; words used in the present tense include the future as well as the present; the words “ insane ” and “ insane person ” shall include every idiot, insane person , and person non compos mentis; the words “ person ” and “whoever” include corporations, companies, associations, firms, partnerships, societies, and joint stock companies, as well as individuals ; “ officer ” includes any person authorized by law to perform the duties of the office; “ signature ” or “ subscription ” includes a mark when the person making the same intended it as such; “ oath ” includes affirmation, and “ sworn ” includes affirmed; “ writing ” includes printing and typewriting and reproductions of visual symbols by photographing, multigraphing, mimeographing, manifolding, or otherwise. (July 30, 1947, ch. 388, 61 Stat. 633 ; June 25, 1948, ch. 645, § 6, 62 Stat. 859 ; Oct. 31, 1951, ch. 655, § 1, 65 Stat. 710 ; Pub. L. 112–231, § 2(a) , Dec. 28, 2012 , 126 Stat. 1619 .) Editorial Notes Amendments 2012— Pub. L. 112–231 , in fifth clause after opening clause, struck out “and ‘lunatic’ ” before “shall include every” and “lunatic,” before “insane person, ”. 1951—Act Oct. 31, 1951 , substituted, in fourth clause after opening clause, “used” for “use”. 1948—Act June 25, 1948 , included “tense”, “whoever”, “signature” , “subscription” , “writing” and a broader definition of “person” . Statutory Notes and Related Subs
perturbation.justification: The modified text redefines 'Representatives' and 'person' in a way that directly conflicts with 1 U.S. Code § 1. The statute explicitly states that 'person' includes corporations, companies, associations, firms, partnerships, societies, and joint stock companies, as well as individuals. The changed text specifically excludes all entities other than individual human beings, significantly narrowing the scope of who can be held liable under the contract, thus creating a legal contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: Participant agrees that Participant will (i) use the Evaluation Material solely for the purposes of the Participation, (ii) not, directly or indirectly, use, disseminate, disclose, discuss or reveal any Evaluation Material, and (iii) otherwise treat and maintain in full confidence all Evaluation Material.
perturbation.changed_text: Participant agrees that Participant will (i) use the Evaluation Material for any purpose it deems necessary, (ii) may, directly or indirectly, use, disseminate, disclose, discuss or reveal any Evaluation Material with prior notice, and (iii) make a reasonable effort to treat and maintain the confidentiality of all Evaluation Material.
perturbation.explanation: The modified text significantly weakens the confidentiality obligations, contradicting the original intent and allowing broad discretion in the use and disclosure of confidential information. The addition of 'reasonable effort' also lowers the standard of care.
perturbation.law_explanation: The Uniform Trade Secrets Act defines misappropriation to include disclosure or use of a trade secret without express or implied consent by a person who knew or had reason to know that his knowledge of the trade secret was acquired under circumstances giving rise to a duty to maintain its secrecy or limit its use. The changed text contradicts this because it allows for dissemination with only prior notice and provides only a reasonable effort to keep confidentiality, as opposed to maintaining secrecy.

perturbation.justification: The changed text significantly weakens confidentiality obligations, contradicting the original intent by allowing dissemination with prior notice and only requiring a reasonable effort to maintain confidentiality. The Law Explanation states that the changed text contradicts the Uniform Trade Secrets Act, which defines misappropriation to include disclosure or use of a trade secret without express or implied consent. While the scraped snippets are unhelpful in determining the contradiction because one is missing and the other is a 404 error, the altered text directly undermines the principle of maintaining secrecy as required under trade secret laws, moving from an obligation to maintain full confidence to merely making 'reasonable effort,' which introduces ambiguity and lowers the standard of care. This weakening of the obligation directly opposes the intent to protect trade secrets, creating a clear legal contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: Participant hereby covenants and agrees that for a period of eighteen months following the date hereof, it shall not, without the prior written consent of Originator (which will not be unreasonably withheld) directly or indirectly: (a) enter into negotiations, provide advice, provide any form of financial assistance (including, without limitation, debt or equity investments) or otherwise encourage, facilitate or consummate any transaction involving Target (whether structured as an investment, a purchase or assets, a purchase of debt or equity interests (including, without limitation, options, warrants or other securities or instruments convertible into or exercisable or exchangeable for debt or equity interests or equity appreciation rights, phantom equity or similar instruments or rights containing equity-like features), a merger, consolidation, combination, recapitalization, refinancing, restructuring, reorganization or otherwise) without the prior express written consent of Originator; or (b) initiate discussions with respect to the prospective employment of, or hire for employment, any employees of Target or any subsidiary thereof.
perturbation.changed_text: Participant hereby agrees that for a period of eighteen months following the date hereof, it shall not, without notifying Originator, engage in discussions regarding possible transactions with Target. This restriction does not apply to transactions involving publicly traded securities of Target.
perturbation.explanation: This change significantly weakens the non-circumvention clause. Replacing the need for 'prior written consent' with simply 'notifying Originator' and explicitly excluding transactions involving publicly traded securities creates a loophole allowing Participant to easily circumvent the intended restrictions.
perturbation.law_explanation: While not a direct contradiction, a non-compete/non-circumvention agreement weakened to the point it becomes unenforceable due to vagueness and loopholes could be interpreted as an unreasonable restraint of trade. If the modification allows the participant to freely engage with the target in ways that undermine the originator's potential transaction and is deemed anticompetitive, it could raise concerns under the Sherman Act, especially if it demonstrably harms market competition. The change from 'prior written consent' to 'notification' is a major shift that weakens the originator's protection.

perturbation.justification: The change weakens the non-circumvention agreement, potentially creating an unreasonable restraint of trade, as explained in the 'Law Explanation'. The original text required 'prior written consent' while the changed text only requires 'notifying Originator' and excludes transactions involving publicly traded securities. This weakening could violate the Sherman Act if deemed anticompetitive. Scraped Law Snippet 1, referencing 15 USC 1, states that 'Every contract, combination in the form of trust or otherwise, or conspiracy, in restraint of trade or commerce among the several States, or with foreign nations, is declared to be illegal.' The change could be interpreted as facilitating actions in restraint of trade, as the originator has reduced ability to prevent unwanted engagements.

perturbation.original_text: Participant acknowledges and affirms that money damages would not be a sufficient remedy for any breach of this Agreement by it and that Originator shall be entitled to seek equitable relief, including injunction and specific performance, as a remedy for any such breach.
perturbation.changed_text: Participant acknowledges that money damages may not be a sufficient remedy for any breach of this Agreement by it, and that equitable relief, including injunction and specific performance, may be considered by Originator as a remedy for such breach.
perturbation.explanation: By replacing 'shall be entitled to seek' with 'may be considered', the modified text makes the availability of equitable relief discretionary rather than mandatory. This weakens Originator's legal recourse and could be interpreted as limiting the court's ability to grant such relief.
perturbation.law_explanation: This section of the Restatement (Second) of Contracts deals with the availability of specific performance. While it doesn't mandate specific performance, it outlines the circumstances under which it is appropriate, particularly when monetary damages are inadequate. By making equitable relief discretionary, the modified text could be interpreted as attempting to preemptively limit the court's power to order specific performance, even when it would otherwise be appropriate under the Restatement. The original text implies that equitable relief will be sought which is a stronger indication to the courts.

perturbation.justification: The original text uses 'shall be entitled to seek', indicating a clear right to equitable relief for the Originator. The changed text replaces this with 'may be considered', making equitable relief discretionary. While the scraped snippets do not directly address this issue, the Perturbation Explanation and Law Explanation both indicate that this change weakens the Originator's legal recourse. The modified text attempts to limit the court's power to order specific performance, even when monetary damages are inadequate. This creates a legal contradiction because it undermines the availability of equitable relief, which could be appropriate under the Restatement (Second) of Contracts as described in the Law Explanation.
